name: Student Life
description:
We're All About You

We are the Division of Student Life. That means we are all about, well, you. Your life as a student; the things you need and the things you want to do—we’re here to help. We deliver health care—physical, mental, dental. We deliver sports and activities and career advice. We deliver leadership training and late night activities. We deliver community, involvement, and support. We deliver a place to rest your (sometimes weary) head and enjoy a meal. We want you to have an excellent student experience—in fact, we expect it. We demand it. And, we'll deliver it. If you need help, just ask and we’ll help you figure it out.
summary: We are the Division of Student Life. That means we are all about, well, you. Your life as a student; the things you need and the things you want to do—we’re here to help.

name: CSI Registered Student Organizations
description:
The CSI’s purpose is to help create a welcoming environment for all University of Oregon students to find community, create lasting memories, persist in school and gain experience for successful careers.

 

Action:

We do our part by supporting student organizations and activities that enliven UO traditions, engage students and connect the campus community. CSI creates and manages student organization support services that accelerate and simplify the event planning process. Services include: the Resource Center space, Supply Nest, Craft Room, Event Supply Exchange, student organization web-based management system, informational tools, storage cubbies, computer access and advising. Our staff support Student Activities Board teams and their free, safe, fun and inspirational events. We encourage student development through intentional trainings and interactions with student employees, leaders and volunteers. CSI develops reoccurring events, organizes crisis support events, assists with campus-wide event planning and collaborates with academic and other campus units.

 

Student Organization Request Forms frequently asked questions (FAQs) about services are located in the documents section below, scroll down to the bottom, click "View All Documents" (on the right side), and open the folder for Student Organizations to see all documents with useful info for Student Orgs.

name: Club Sports
description:
Club teams practice, host games and events, travel to competitions throughout the US, and contend for national championships every year. Currently there are 41 club teams with more than 1,400 participants.

Club Sports is a program in the Division of Student Life; teams are not organizationally affiliated with the Department of Intercollegiate Athletics, nor sanctioned by the NCAA.

name: Fraternity & Sorority Life
description:
Fraternity men and sorority women at UO have a strong commitment to philanthropy and community service. Members of the organizations continue to demonstrate this commitment through their participation in various philanthropic events throughout the year.

Membership in a fraternity or sorority can be a great way to become involved in the rich traditions at UO and create life-long friendships. Please look through the information on our web site, it may answer some questions that you have. If you still have any questions, feel free to contact our staff. We are here to help you and your student have a great UO experience.

Should you have any questions, do not hesitate to contact our staff. We are more than happy to address any questions or concerns you may have. We can be reached at (541) 346-2912 or GoGreek@uoregon.edu.
summary: Today, there are 34 recognized fraternities and sororities at UO for students to choose from. This is a proud heritage that students can become part of by becoming a member of one of our recognized organizations.

name: Japanese Student Association
description:
Regardless of nationality, race, or creed, people have dreams. These dreams, and the paths we take in life may one day lead someone to Japan. Above all though, the members of the Japanese Student Association are citizens of the earth. We work to not only improve ourselves and the community but the world as well. The Japanese Student Association (JSA) was established by University of Oregon students to support Japanese students in their endeavors in the United States, as well as to introduce Japanese language and culture to non-Japanese students and community members interested in world cultures.

JSA holds bi-weekly meetings throughout the school year as well as special events like our annual "Japan Night" culture showcase celebration that draws in over 300 guests each year! Our meetings and activities encompass a large variety of themes such as Japanese and American food and culture, language, traditions, current events, Japanese movie nights, and much more! All are welcome to join! 

Be sure to follow us on Instagram for additional updates and ways to get involved as well! We hope to meet you in the near future!
summary: The UO Japanese Student Association holds bi-weekly meetings throughout the school year, as well as special events like our annual "Japan Night" cultural showcase celebration! All are welcome and make sure to follow our Instagram for additional updates!

name: Graduate Ecology and Evolutionary Biology Students
description:
We are the organization of the graduate students in the Institute of Ecology and Evolution.

Our mission:

1) Enhance graduate student communication and relationships with the other graduate students, the Biology Faculty, and the GTFF.

2) Encourage academic leadership among biology graduate students by hosting seminar speakers in ecology and evolutionary biology, subsidizing student research and travel to scientific meetings, offering workshops to advise undergraduates considering graduate degrees in the life sciences, and providing research funds for undergraduate research projects under GrEBES mentorship.

3) Provide graduate students, post-doctoral fellows, and undergraduates with career-building advice through a series of informal workshops or seminars.

4) Participate in community service and outreach programs outside of the UO campus.

name: Green Business Initiative Student Association
description: The Green Business Initiative Student Association (GBISA) is one of the first student organizations of its kind. Comprised of graduate students from the University of Oregon School of Law and the Lundquist College of Business, GBISA members are interested in and dedicated to the intersection of law, business, and the environment. GBISA’s mission is to promote awareness of sustainable business practices and the legal framework and policies that support green business. GBISA’s leading activity each year is to organize its annual Green Business Symposium at the White Stag Block in Portland, Oregon. The Symposium aims to be the premier student-organized green business conference in the Pacific Northwest. The Symposium brings together hundreds of regional and national business leaders, policymakers, legal practitioners, academics, and students to engage in discussions on how we as a society can achieve a more sustainable economy and living environment.

Come join us for weekly meetings, AKA (Ading-Kuya-Ate) program, and more fun events throughout the year (Fil-AM, Pasko, and PCN)

name: Chi Psi
description: Chi Psi is a private fraternity of gentlemen devoted to the personal and collective needs of its members. A selective association of diverse individuals, Chi Psi is dedicated to the cultivation of a unique environment which instills a lifelong commitment to brotherhood. Founded in 1841 at Union College in New York, Chi Psi is the United States’ eighth oldest fraternity and the first fraternity founded on principles of brotherhood. Maintaining a strong national presence with chapters (known as “Alphas”) at 32 top universities nationwide, Chi Psis continue to be some of the highest achievers in the nation both as an undergraduate and in the many diverse paths Chi Psis pursue after their academic career is over. About Eta Delta: Alpha Eta Delta is one of Oregon’s oldest fraternities and has had an active Alpha since 1921. Located at 1018 Hilyard St less than two blocks from campus, the “Lodge”, as all Chi Psi houses are known, is a great place for brothers to live and operate on a day-to-day basis. Eta Deltas pride themselves on their ability to not just be good fraternity men, but men that take the time to better themselves in every area of living. To find out more, come stop by!

name: Pocket Playhouse
description:
The Pocket is a student run organization that helps students craft their artistic visions on the stage. Each quarter, the Pocket produces up to 2 shows per term, completely under the direction and operation of students. Many productions are original, some are new renderings of old classics, and some are obscure offerings seldom seen on stage, but they all come from the vision of directors, actors and designers just like you! The Pocket Playhouse aims to provide an arena for students to get involved in all aspects of theatre, regardless of prior experience. We believe that no amount of classroom time can offer a student the opportunity to learn what hands-on experience offers by definition. Every student who desires it should have a place, a time, and an opportunity for that kind of experience. The Pocket remains an open venue for any and every student seeking the opportunity to pursue their creative vision in the Art of Theatre.

This meant starting from the ground-up and progressing towards becoming better men and developing an organization of excellence. The Founding Fathers of Gamma Zeta Chapter were dissatisfied with the stereotypical image of a college fraternity, and in joining Sigma Nu vowed to build something they could be proud of; creating something greater than themselves, based on the values of Love, Honor and Truth. Our Chapter's principles reflect the collective image of our members, and we strive to live in accordance with our guiding principles.

name: Taiwanese Student Association
description:
The mission of the Taiwanese Students Association (TWSA) is to coordinate academic, social, and cultural activities for Taiwanese students and other University of Oregon students. We are dedicated to assisting students with Taiwanese backgrounds to adjust their life in the United States. We also strive to create a welcoming community between international and domestic students as well as Taiwanese and non-Taiwanese students. To do so, we create opportunities to enhance the relationship between TWSA members and the community by presenting cultural events and activities that stimulate a better understanding of Taiwanese culture and add cultural diversity at the University of Oregon. 

 

Join us for our meetings every Tuesday in Gerlinger 302 at 6PM! Follow our instagram for more information: @uo_twsa

name: National Society of Leadership and Success (Sigma Alpha Pi)
description:
The NSLS is the largest and only accredited leadership honor society in the United States. Our local chapter is part of this national organization with more than 800 chapters and over 2 million existing members.

Students are selected based on exemplary academic achievement and leadership potential. Our chapter members will have access to:

  • Over $400,000 in annual scholarship opportunities (visit nsls.org/scholarships-and-awards to learn more)

  • An exclusive NSLS job bank

  • A personalized letter of recommendation

 
Our leadership program teaches interpersonal skills that give you an advantage in a competitive job market. If you become a member, you can also:

  • Learn from top global leaders like former US Presidents, CEOs, celebrities, and more with exclusive Speaker Broadcasts.

  • Meet new friends and make lifelong connections (on-campus and virtually).

  • Overcome challenges with success coaches, shortcutting your way to your goals.

  • Gain access to exclusive private networking events.

    Visit nsls.org to learn more and accept your nomination. Frequently Asked Questions:

    Some common questions we receive are listed below. If you have any other questions, visit nsls.org to chat with a team member or email them at support@nsls.org.

    What do I have to do to become inducted?

    The Steps to Induction take approximately 9-10 hours over the course of the semester and you can complete it at your own pace. To learn more, visit nsls.org/the-membership-experience.

    Is this recognized on campus?

    The NSLS is officially recognized on our campus and is led by Wren Harwell (they/them/theirs), Vice-President . Student members in our chapter will join more than 2 million members nationwide.
summary: The NSLS is the largest and only accredited leadership honor society in the United States. Our local chapter is part of this national organization with more than 800 chapters and over 2 million existing members.

Alpha Chi Omega is a member of the National Panhellenic Conference.

name: Divisi
description: Divisi is the premier treble a cappella singing group from the University of Oregon. Constantly striving to achieve the highest quality of music and performance, Divisi has become one of the most well-known and admired collegiate groups in the nation. Founded in 2001, Divisi has toured the country, competed in the International Championship of Collegiate A Cappella, and recorded multiple highly praised albums. Fun fact: the movie Pitch Perfect was based on the group that went to compete in the International Championship of Collegiate A Cappella in 2002! Divisi also continues to perform for free every Friday afternoon on the University of Oregon campus, as well as perform all over the state of Oregon. Divisi holds open auditions every Fall and every Spring term. If you are interested in auditioning, please check our Instagram, or email us for more details.

name: JIU JITSU
description:
The UO Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu (BJJ) club is a co-ed UO Club Sports team open to everyone, from casual beginners to avid competitors! We facilitate a friendly and encouraging environment for people to learn self-defense and be part of an uplifting community. Keep up to date by following @uo.jiujitsu on Instagram.

 

Schedule (always in the Gerling Hall room 219):

Club Days (all skill levels, no-gi): Tuesday 7:00-9:00, Thursday 7:00-9:00 

Open Mat (all skill levels, gi optional): Sunday 1:30-2:30

Women's hour (all skill levels): Sunday 12:30-1:30

Comp team practice (for those interested in taking the sport to the next level): Friday 8:30-10:30 and Sundays 5:30-7:30 

 

 

Recommended Equipment:

A rash guard, compression top, or tight T-shirt (long, baggy clothes will get damaged and are a safety risk)

Shorts and/or leggings

A mouthguard

Optional: A gi, the traditional clothing worn during BJJ, is optional on certain non "Club" days (see schedule above). UO BJJ is primarily a no-gi club.

 

 

Club Officers:

President: Kieran Watts (Kieranwa@uoregon.edu)

Vice President: Adi Lage (alage@uoregon.edu)

Social Media Manager: Phoenix Nwokedi (pnwokedi@uoregon.edu)

Outreach Officer: Andy Wilten (awilten@uoregon.edu)

Merchandise Officer: Sid Lefranc (sile@uoregon.edu)

Treasurer: Will Wofford (wwofford@uoregon.edu)
summary: The UO Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu (BJJ) club is a co-ed UO Club Sports team open to everyone, from casual beginners to avid competitors! We facilitate a friendly and encouraging environment for people to learn self-defense and be part of an uplifting community.

name: Net Impact Undergraduate Chapter
description:
We are the University of Oregon’s Undergraduate Chapter of Net Impact. Net Impact is a global network of over 435 chapters and 100,000 emerging leaders using the power of business to create a more just and sustainable world.

We empower students to navigate the intersection of business, social responsibility, and sustainability by facilitating networking trips, seminars, and social impact projects. We develop our members’ professional skills and their expertise on sustainable business practices while creating a tight-knit community of like-minded leaders.

Net Impact is housed in the Lundquist College of Business, but we are open to students from all academic backgrounds. We also ensure free membership for all of our members thanks to our partners—the Center for Sustainable Business Practices and Associated Students of the University of Oregon. Our general meetings take place every Thursday at 6:00 PM in Lillis 245.

description:
The Queer Ally Coalition (QAC) seeks to reduce homophobia, heterosexism, and gender bias on the University of Oregon campus and create an atmosphere of understanding and acceptance for all members of our community inclusive of all sexual orientations and gender identities. In addition to visibly identifying members of our community who are willing to be acknowledged as allies for gay, lesbian, bisexual, transgender, and questioning students, faculty, and staff, we organize training programs and provide ongoing opportunities for dialogue to expand our understanding of the issues in our campus community.

PRSSA is made up of more than 10,000 students and advisers organized into 300 plus Chapters in the United States and one in Argentina. We are headquartered in New York City, and led by a National Committee of PRSSA and PRSA members.

Our mission is (1) to raise awareness on campus about disabilities and disability culture, (2) to advocate for equal access in physical, academic, and social environments, and (3) to have fun and support one another. Our activities include holding regular meetings to plan and socialize, organizing disability awareness events that involve films, panels, or cultural activities, and inviting locally and nationally recognized speakers with disabilities to UO to share their experiences. We would love for you to join us!

description: The University of Oregon Financial Management Association (FMA) is a student-run organization designed to educate students on the different fields of finance within the business world. Our main goal is to help students determine if the field of finance is the one for them. We meet on even-numbered weeks of every term in Lillis 262 from 6-7 pm.  Our meetings’ agendas will include guest speakers sharing their successes and experiences in the financial world, workshops to better prepare students for real-life situations, and hands-on activities to help club members interact with one another. Further activities and events we will offer include site visits and networking seminars to expose students to prominent companies and help them interact with professionals. FMA strives to maintain and raise the level of success that this program has reached over the years.

name: Economics Club
description:
The University of Oregon Economics Club is a community for undergraduate and graduate students, faculty, and community members with an interest in economics. Our weekly meetings feature guest speakers and opportunities for discussions on current events and economics outside of a classroom setting. 

 

Through executive-led meetings, lectures from alternative departments, and other informal activities, UO Economics Club members have the chance to strengthen their connections with faculty and other students in the department.

 

Our mission is driven by a commitment to academic excellence and supportive community relationships. You’ll have a chance to enrich your education by meeting new friends, learning from professors in and outside of economics, and engaging in original and independent research about economics.

 

We’re active on Instagram and Twitter (@uoeconomicsclub), so check us out there!

 

Questions? Email us at uoeconclub@gmail.com.
summary: The University of Oregon Economics Club is a community for students, faculty, and community members with an interest in economics. Our weekly meetings feature guest speakers and other opportunities for student engagement outside of a classroom setting.

Our mission is to help you develop long-term career goals and strategies, facilitate self-exploration and discovery, connect with potential employers, and empower and challenge you to fulfill your potential. We look forward to serving as your advocate as you pursue an inspired and fulfilling future. 

The UO Career Center is a part of the Division of Student Life and has offices in Hendricks Hall on the UO campus and in the White Stag Block at the University of Oregon in Portland.

If you are interested in learning how to dance hula, getting involved in a group with a melting pot of ethnicities, playing intramural sports, or just wanting to know more about Hawaiʻi, our email is mahalo@uoregon.edu or you can follow us on Instagram at @uohuiohawaii.

name: ULTIMATE-Women's
description:
Oregon women's ultimate has existed since the mid 80s, and holds a long history of success. They placed first at USAU Nationals in 2010 and 2013, 2nd in 2014, and 3rd in 2009, along with multiple other appearances in more recent years. We have two Callahan winner: Jody Dozono in 1999 and Chelsea Dengler in 2003, and Julia Sherwood was a finalist in 2010. Fugue has a history of fielding many USA Junior World’s team members, and we have been successful at major tournaments year-round such as the Stanford Invite, President’s Day, and Santa Barbara Invite. We are a tight-knit group who love our sport, and want to welcome as many new teammates as we can.

Fugue /fyüg/ noun 1 a dream-like state of loss of one’s identity that is usually coupled with flight from one’s usual environment. 2 a musical term to describe a short melody introduced by one part and successively taken up by others.

name: RUNNING
description: UO Running Club provides the opportunity for students to get together to run, train and race. Most of our sessions are steady runs. A couple of days each week we offer harder training sessions (intervals, repetitions, hills, and tempo runs). The club is coached by Tom Heinonen, a member of the US Track and Field and Cross Country Hall of Fame and the Ducks Athlectic Hall of Fame, who coached the University of Oregon women for nearly three decades. In his time coaching the women, he transformed the team from a brand new program into a NCAA powerhouse. He retired in 2003 to start the club and has been coaching it since. In the Fall, we compete in collegiate cross country races throughout Oregon, then finish our season at the NIRCA Championships, where we face other college clubs. In the Spring, we enter in collegiate track meets. Several of our runners earn the opportunity to race at Hayward Field throughout the year. There are road races throughout the year. We have a wide range of talent and commitment levels, from recreational runners to All-Americans. Club members receive an e-mailed workout schedule for the following week every Sunday. There is no fee to join the club. All running sessions are optional. To join, just show up to one of the practices or contact one of us. The Running Club is a great way to get together with students and to enjoy running!

name: Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Inc.
description: Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity, Inc. (ΑΦΑ) is the oldest intercollegiate historically African American fraternity. It was initially a literary and social studies club organized in the 1905–1906 school year at Cornell University but later evolved into a fraternity with a founding date of December 4, 1906. It employs an icon from Ancient Egypt, the Great Sphinx of Giza, as its symbol. Its aims or pillars are "Manly Deeds, Scholarship, and Love For All Mankind," and its motto is "First of All, Servants of All, We Shall Transcend All."

description:
Club Softball is a self-funded, student-run organization at the University of Oregon, consisting of 17 dedicated members who compete in the National Club Softball Association (NCSA) within the Pacific Region – North Conference.

Our season runs year-round, beginning with tryouts in the fall and continuing with games throughout the academic year. We are a competitive team that practices multiple times each week, emphasizing both athletic excellence and academic success. Each member is self-motivated, hardworking, and committed to upholding the high standards of our program.

Our ultimate goal is to qualify for postseason play, including the Pacific Regional Playoffs in late April and the NCSA World Series in mid-May.

name: Unbound Journal
description: Unbound is a student-run literary journal at the University of Oregon that publishes undergraduate and graduate student work in the areas of prose, poetry and visual art. The journal was founded in 2008 as a Clark Honors College thesis project and has evolved into a biannual online publication. Our mission is to foster the development of all students at the University of Oregon, regardless of major, by serving as a platform for outstanding creative expression. Unbound Journal is committed to the belief that publishing a community’s literature is a crucial component to sustaining a vibrant culture. We publish poetry, fiction, nonfiction, and visual art that tests boundaries and comes from a place of passion, regardless of medium or approach. Our editorial process values quality as the paramount criterion. Each submission receives feedback and critique in a blind review from our staff of student editors. Unbound seeks to showcase the Oregon community’s mosaic of talent and experience. You can find more out at unboundjournal.wordpress.com, at our Instagram at @unboundjournal, and access the digital archive at https://issuu.com/unbound.

name: Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual, Trans, Queer Alliance
description:
Welcome to the LGBTQA3 (commonly shortened to QA3)!! Located in EMU 022, we're usually open 10-5 Monday-Friday (with some small changes according to availability). Updated hours will be posted on our door the first week of the term and when we're open we have a whiteboard outside with upcoming events listed!

 

We're a great place to meet new people, drink some tea, explore our queer library, or do some homework! We've got lots of crafting supplies and events that go on, including hosting many different identity subgroups such as Lesbian Legion, Gay Brigade, Bi and Beyond, Trans and Nonbinary, and Aces and Aros.

 

We also host weekly general meetings on a regular basis. This is a great place to get involved with the organization, learn about different resources and events, contribute to planning events like our annual drag show and queer prom, or just get to know other LGBTQIA+ people on campus!

 

We don't regularly update our Facebook, so the best way to get in contact with us is through email, Instagram, or coming into the space!

 

If you're interested in hosting an identity subgroup you don't see listed, feel free to email us at uolgbtqa3@gmail.com or message us on Instagram @lgbtqa3!

 

 

The LGBTQIA3 Alliance’s core purpose is to create opportunities for people to celebrate, appreciate, and affirm their whole selves. Our mission is to prevent isolation while raising awareness and maintaining a space inclusive to all sexual orientations, gender identities, and expressions. Our vision is of a world free from homophobia, heterosexism, and gender bias. Our office serves as a student-led organization to host weekly meetings, plan annual events, house a lending library, and provide resources, support, and education to queer members of the UO and Eugene communities. Please join us if you are interested in learning more about the LGBTQA or are interested in connecting with other LGBTQA members and UO students.
summary: The LGBTQIA3 Alliance’s core purpose is to create opportunities for people to celebrate, appreciate, and affirm their whole selves. Our mission is to prevent isolation while raising awareness and maintaining a space inclusive to all queer identities

name: Phi Gamma Delta
description: Phi Gamma Delta (ΦΓΔ), commonly known as Phi Gam and sometimes written as FIJI, is a North American social fraternity with 139 active chapters and 13 colonies across the United States and Canada. It was founded at Jefferson College, Pennsylvania, in 1848. Along with Phi Kappa Psi, Phi Gamma Delta forms half of the Jefferson Duo. Since its founding, the fraternity has initiated more than 211,000 brothers.
summary: Friendship, knowledge, service, morality, and excellence... scholarship, fraternity, and self.

name: Coalition Against Environmental Racism
description: CAER is a student organization committed to bringing to light the connections between the struggles for social and environmental equality. CAER has the dual mission of educating the campus community and the Eugene community at large about Environmental Racism and building networks within the University and Eugene to foster Woke community, engagement in activism for the Liberation Movement by means of eco-reclamation, eco-education, eco-abolition, and eco-decolonization. Environmental racism emphasizes that exploited communities of color are targeted by racist governmental policies, and private interests, which refuse to involve them in decision-making processes. Thus, our environmental justice serves to acknowledge and work towards addressing the immediate and long-term effects upon communities of color, low income people, and other exploited groups, from: war, bioweapons, pollution, waste disposal, hazardous sites, resource depletion, and natural disasters due to climate change.
summary: CAER is a student organization committed to bridging the gap between the struggle for social and environmental equality. CAER has the dual mission of educating the campus about Environmental Racism and building coalitions within the University communit...

name: Student Activities Board
description:
The Student Activities Board is a student organization dedicated to the creation of on-campus events. From guest lectures and film screenings, to live music events and game nights, the board serves the campus community by creating inclusive events by UO students, for UO students.

Purpose: Serving the UO Student Population by promoting positive community through resources, interests, and opportunities.

Action: We do this by supporting fellow Student Activities Board student leaders through specific reoccurring weekly programming, Trivia and Bingo, Ducks After Dark Movies, and music based programming.

description: NALSA endeavors to promote the study of Indian Law and traditional forms of government while recruiting, supporting and maintaining students pursuing Indian law.
summary: NALSA endeavors to promote the study of Indian Law and traditional forms of government while recruiting, supporting and maintaining students pursuing Indian law.

name: Oregon Review of International Law
description: The Oregon Review of International Law (ORIL) is a student-run journal of the University of Oregon School of Law. ORIL publishes articles by academics, practitioners, and students that address current legal topics in international law and policy. International law plays an increasingly significant role in domestic law, politics, and everyday life. An understanding and appreciation of international law is necessary for a complete understanding of the national legal landscape. As the volumes of international treaties continue to grow, ORIL seeks to publish timely articles that describe the legal aspects of these treaties and the responsibilities they impose on human rights, taxation, the environment, and a wide variety of other international legal topics.
summary: The Oregon Review of International Law (ORIL) is a student-run journal of the University of Oregon School of Law. ORIL publishes articles by academics, practitioners, and students that address current legal topics in international law and policy.

name: BADMINTON
description: Welcome! We are a club that is dedicated to creating a positive environment and showcasing the fun and competitive nature of badminton to UO members (staff and students only)! Whether you're a first time player or longtime enjoyer of the sport, we would love to have you come try out the club. While we don't offer special training, we do host competitions and are working on developing the  more advanced side of our club. Fret not though if you're a beginner! We may not offer formal training but if it's your first time with a racket, our members won't hesitate to lend a hand. Meetings are every Monday/Wednesday 8:00-11:00pm and Saturday 2:00-5:00 in Gerlinger Annex.
summary: Welcome to UO Badminton Club! Built only for UO students and staff members, we foster a positive environment for both competitive and recreational play! We meet every Monday/Wednesday 7:00-10:00pm and Saturday 2:00-5:00 in Gerlinger Annex.

name: Delta Gamma
description: Delta Gamma (ΔΓ), commonly known as DG, is a North American sorority, although they often still use the term "women's fraternity." It was established in 1873 at the Lewis School in Oxford, Mississippi. It has 151 collegiate chapters and more than 200 alumnae groups. The organization's executive office is in Columbus, Ohio.[1] Delta Gamma was one of seven charter members of the National Panhellenic Conference.

name: Association of Fundraising Professionals UO Collegiate Chapter
description:
The Association of Fundraising Professionals (AFP) works to advance philanthropy through advocacy, research, education and certification programs. The association fosters development and growth of fundraising professionals and promotes high ethical standards in the fundraising profession. 

The AFP UO student group works to supplement the education of students with discussions, a speaker series, and real-world experience working with a local nonprofit. 

We meet weekly in a hybrid format at Hendricks Hall, room 006C, and online.

name: Kappa Delta (Alpha Lambda)
description: Kappa Delta (ΚΔ, also known as KD or Kaydee) is an American collegiate social sorority. Established in 1897, it was the first sorority founded at the State Female Normal School (now Longwood University), in Farmville, Virginia. Kappa Delta is one of the "Farmville Four", four now national sororities that were established at the university. It is a member of the National Panhellenic Conference.
summary: Kappa Delta Sorority is committed to providing opportunities and experiences that inspire women to greatness.

name: Nontraditional Student Union
description:
Nontraditional Student Union (NSU)

We welcome students from all backgrounds and life situations. The NSU is your community, helping you find a sense of belonging at the University of Oregon. We serve:

  • First-generation college students
  • Parents and caregivers
  • Transfer students
  • Students over 24
  • Veterans
  • And many others

Resources We Provide

  • Study space and computers
  • Free printing
  • Basic school supplies
  • Resource board
  • Community connections and engagement
  • Snacks and refreshments

Our Mission

We advocate for nontraditional students' unique needs and accommodations, recognizing that each student's journey is different. The University of Oregon is a large institution, and we're dedicated to helping fellow students navigate it successfully while achieving their academic goals.

Club meetings held first and third Friday of each month at noon. Office hours updated and posted on NSU office door. 

  • Monday:  12-2:30

    Tuesday: 2-4:30

    Wednesday: 9-11:30, 12-2:30

    Thursday:  12-2:30

    Friday: 10-12:30

Important Note: As nontraditional students ourselves, our hours may vary due to family responsibilities and other obligations. Any schedule changes will be announced promptly on our Instagram and Engage platforms.

Our space is open to all students. We believe in students supporting students, so stop by to study, grab a snack, or connect with your peers!

description: MISSION & GOALS STATEMENT The Native American Student Union (NASU) at the University of Oregon assists American Indian, Alaskan Natives, and Indigenous Peoples in maintaining cultural values while pursuing their educational goals. NASU emphasizes the support of, the safety of, and the educational success of the Native American community. NASU is also actively involved in recruitment and retention of both High School and College Native American students. The NASU organization seeks to treat all people fairly and equally within the organization as well as within the community. Diversity is respected within the Native community as well as outside of it. NASU maintains an alcohol and drug free ethic. WHO WE ARE/WHAT WE DO The Native American Student Union is a multiethnic group comprised of Native Students from different nations as well as allies. The Native American Student Union works with tribes as well as schools/organizations in urban centers to maintain cultural awareness. Our organization holds many events throughout the school year, and these events promote the cultural development of our campus, as well as recruit future students. We promote health awareness, a space to develop identity and safe space. HISTORICAL SUMMARY The Native American Student Union is one of the oldest groups on campus, as well as one of the smallest communities. We struggle to recruit and retain Native students to campus via programming. It is very important that Indigenous communities as well as the majority population understand issues that indigenous people of this land are facing.

The goal of SELF is to expand students understanding of the issues and dynamics involved with sports and entertainment law through guest speakers, sports and entertainment law forums and discussions, and organizing the largest sports and entertainment law conference in the Northwest.

name: Student Government Engagement and Success
description:
Student Government Engagement and Success (“SGES”) empowers students to become future community leaders by providing experiential opportunities in governance, self-advocacy, and leadership. We strive to cultivate a campus culture where student advocacy and participation drive skill development and contribute to continuous institutional improvement.

Internal Impact

  1. Personalized Support: We provide tailored advising, coaching, and support to ASUO Officers, student organization leaders, and students, helping them navigate university processes and strengthen their self-advocacy skills.
  2. Educational Programs: We design and deliver education, leadership development, and continuity programs that equip students with the knowledge and tools to advocate effectively for themselves and their communities.
  3. Administrative Oversight: We manage the administrative and financial support services of ASUO, ensuring student initiatives adhere to university and ASUO-managed policies, rules, and standards as well as state and federal law and safety standards.
  4. Career Readiness: We offer meaningful work experiences that build career-ready skills for students employed within our department.

Institutional Impact

  1. Student Engagement: We champion the value of student engagement by educating the campus community on its contributions to university life and advocating for its ongoing support.
  2. Leadership Continuity: We strengthen the continuity of student government leadership by providing transitional support, particularly during ASUO’s annual elections, empowering students to pursue their goals and advocate for their needs.
  3. Compliance and Alignment: We ensure that student-led initiatives align with institutional standards and comply with state and federal regulations, promoting accountability and sustainability.
summary: We empower students to become future leaders through experiential opportunities in governance, self-advocacy, and leadership.

name: DucksVets (fka Veterans and Family Student Association)
description:
DucksVets was formed to create a social space for student veterans, dependents, and military-affiliated members within the University of Oregon where the commonalities of military experience could be shared. Our mission includes empowering veteran and dependent students and providing network opportunities, resources, advocacy, and service to the university and surrounding community. The DuckVets is committed to providing experiences that elevate the academic, personal, and professional development of all students at the University of Oregon.  Today, the DuckVets is open to ALL University of Oregon students who share an appreciation for the history, culture, and sacrifice made by UO student veterans. 

Next leadership meeting: 10/24 (open to DuckVet members)
summary: The VFSA was formed to create a social space for student veterans and military-affiliated members within the University of Oregon. VFSA is open to ALL U of O students who share an appreciation for the sacrifice made by UO student veterans.

name: Mills International Center
description:
Meet the World Here!

We are a lounge, resource center, event venue, and a home away from home! We are a great place to spend time if you enjoy traveling and good company, are an international student, have been or will go on study abroad, or simply are looking for a comfy couch. 

We have a lending library full of travel and culture guides (books and DVDs), cookbooks, and travel narratives, as well as magazines and CDs from around the world. And the Mills is never a dull place to be with its popularity as a place to host events. Some examples of past events are art exhibitions, movie nights, and study abroad information sessions. 

Mills Center is a great place to make new friends from all over the world. No matter what you may be looking for, the Mills International Center has something to offer you.

description: Eugene, Oregon's only LIVE shadow cast of the Rocky Horror Picture Show! We invite you once again to watch us perform a shadow cast of one of the greatest cult films of all time! name: Oregon Ballroom Dance Club
description:
Every Friday night of the term, you'll find us in Gerlinger Hall (rm 220) from 6:30-9:00. We begin with a beginner-friendly lesson of a single kind of ballroom dance, then at 7:30 we switch to social dancing.

 

During our social dance, we change dance styles every song, but not to worry! For anyone unsure of how to do a certain dance, we have our Beginner Box where, during the first half hour of social dance, we teach the basic moves to anyone who would like to learn. We want to make everyone feel as comfortable and welcome as possible, especially beginners!

 

The dances we include most evenings are: Waltz, Tango, Foxtrot, Quickstep, Viennese Waltz, Rumba, Cha-cha, Samba, Salsa, East Coast Swing, West Coast Swing, Argentine Tango, Bachata, Hustle, Night Club Two Step and Merengue.

Occasionally, we include dances like Jive, Pasodoble, and Polka.

 

Our dances are completely free, and we're very excited to introduce more people to the world of ballroom dance. See you on Friday!

Join our discord at: https://discord.gg/hfzsNDdcBy

If you'd like to join our email list, please submit a response to the form "Email List" below. Watch your emails for updates on the dances we'll be teaching throughout the term.
summary: We host a free social dance every Friday night on campus! We start at 6:30 pm in Gerlinger Hall (rm 220) with an hour long beginner lesson, then a social dance until 9:00 pm. We are beginner-friendly and encourage dancers, new and old, to check us out!

description: The Dean’s Council for Inter-Club Coordination (DCICC) serves as the governing body for all Lundquist College of Business (LCB) student organizations. name: International Business and Economics Club
description:
The University of Oregon International Business and Economics Club (IBEC) is a student organization devoted to the Professional and Social development of globally-minded individuals and the creation of a close community network of future leaders within the Lundquist College of Business and all other schools at the University of Oregon.

 

What We Do:
- Networking: We organize events, such as our annual black-tie dinner, where students can meet and learn from professionals from various industries.
- Education: Through workshops, guest lectures, and seminars, we provide our members with the knowledge and skills necessary to excel in international markets.
- Career Development: We assist our members in preparing for career opportunities by offering resume workshops, interview preparation sessions, and internship referrals through our position within the LCB.
- Social Events: We aim to balance professional growth with personal enjoyment by hosting a variety of social events to foster a sense of community and help members build lasting friendships. These events offer opportunities for members to connect, share experiences, and enjoy the diverse cultural backgrounds represented within our club.

 

Join Us:
Whether you are interested in global markets or just looking to connect with like-minded students at UO, IBEC offers valuable experiences and a welcoming community. We have no dues and no requirements, so anyone is welcome to stop by our meetings. Follow us on Instagram and Linkedin to stay updated on our events and learn how you can get involved.

name: ASUO Departments Finance Committee

description: The UO Hip Hop Team is the University of Oregon's first hip hop based dance team. We are non-competitive and an ASUO group. The UO Hip Hop Team was formally official in 2006 in hopes to act as another form of an extracurricular dance activity for UO college students. We have danced at a variety of events including UO basketball games, The Venus Festival, Greek events, high school dance competitions, pre-performances at UO Duck football games, and numerous NBA Portland TrailBlazer games. The team was created as an extracurricular/non-competitive hip hop dance team in order to enhance school spirit and to give students the opportunity to get involved, gain friends and peers, work toward success in school, and lead a healthy lifestyle. Don't forget, the UO Hip Hop Team was also established to dance! UO HHT's purpose is to provide an activity that is less time consuming, easy going and fun so those busy students have time to work and play, while still being involved. The Head Coach is Taylor Hunter. The Assistant Coach is Rachel Monteagudo. Team Captain is Kaitlyn Stattler. The Social Media Managers are Kierra Navarro & Sydney Mercer.

name: Rehearsals for Life
description:
Rehearsals for Life was started to help people learn and practice stepping in and defusing disrespectful situations. The troupe, made up of UO graduate students, uses their own stories and experiences to find inspiration for their performances and discuss issues of power and privilege. But these are not just ordinary theater performances; audience members are invited to participate in the performances and practice navigating potentially disrespectful situations. Through these performances and workshops, Rehearsals for Life works to build healthier relationships and a more equitable campus for all.

Related to Rehearsals for Life is the Intertwined Personal Storytelling Project. Intertwined is produced by the Division of Equity and Inclusion and aims at increasing Love, Authenticity, Courage, and Empathy (LACE) on campus through storytelling events. On November 21st there will be a storytelling workshop held at McKenzie Hall 101 from 4:00 to 6:00 pm. Later in May 2025, there will be the Intertwined Storytelling Performances.

name: Radical Organizing and Activism Resource (ROAR) Center
description:
Located in EMU 006 

OFFICE HOURS

  • Alex(a) Wed 1:30-3:30
  • Riya: Thurs 2-4 pm 
  • Blaise:  Wed 3-5 pm
  • Myna: Tues 12-2pm
  • Cole:  Mon 12-2pm
  • Marie: Thurs 11:00-1:00
  • Yvette:  Tues 1:30-3:30

 

The ROAR is a student-run coalition at the University of Oregon dedicated to fostering a transformative community through radical political engagement. 

Since the 1970s, we have served as a physical hub and resource center for leftist organizations, providing essential materials and support for campaigns aimed at dismantling systems of oppression, especially capitalism, imperialism, colonialism, racism, sexism, and homophobia. We strive to cultivate a university rooted in community, compassion, cooperation, and creativity, where students can collaborate, organize, and thrive politically and artistically. Through workshops, events, and shared resources, we empower both seasoned activists and newcomers alike to imagine and work towards a just and sustainable now for all.
summary: The ROAR Center is resource space where individuals can build community, attend meetings, borrow books from our library, engage in direct actions and campaigns planned for the year, define their radical politics, and get to know other like-minded folks!

Members also have the chance to gain great camaraderie and strengthen their leadership skills. The UO AMA is open to all students, regardless of major, who are interested in marketing.

The UO Real Estate Investment Group is a student organization of undergraduate students who learn about the components of real estate investing, pitch real investment properties and explore different facets of commercial/residential real estate. To ensure knowledge is not just limited to members, UOREIG regularly invites guest speakers anywhere from developers to investment bankers to share their knowledge and experience. Similarly, the group tours a few sites per term that may be local apartment construction sites, retail rental space, or anything in between to gain some more hands-on experience. Everyone in the club also participates in case studies and property pitches to a local investor, as we aim to gain several soft skills along the way such as public speaking and rhetoric. To foster a community, the group hosts social events for our members to create strong friendships and connections with each other. The Real Estate Investment Group's goal is to facilitate learning about real-world real estate investing skills that are not taught elsewhere at the University of Oregon and to guide students towards the many possible careers in commercial real estate while making lifelong connections along the way.

name: ASUO Constitution Court
description:
The ASUO Judicial Branch, also known as the Constitution Court, serves as the guardian of ASUO integrity and accountability, ensuring compliance with ASUO rules and policies. Responsible for policy interpretation and officer compliance, the Judicial Branch responds to inquiries, resolves disputes, and applies ASUO regulations to specific cases. Advised by the Director of Student Government Engagement and Success, it operates with a five-member team of Associate Justices.

This regulatory body holds the authority to interpret rules, determine constitutionality, and apply the law to the actions of ASUO officers and governmental bodies. It also has the power to impose sanctions to ensure compliance with its decisions. The court includes at least two students enrolled in the UO Law Degree program, with all Justices serving as long as they remain enrolled University of Oregon students who meet officer eligibility requirements.
summary: The ASUO Judicial Branch, also known as the Constitution Court, ensures ASUO integrity and accountability by interpreting policies, resolving disputes, and enforcing compliance with ASUO rules.

Members get to talk with professionals and other students, tour campus and community buildings, and see firsthand the inner workings of buildings that keep people comfortable and safe-places you never get a chance to see otherwise!  ASHRAE also provides travel opportunities around the US and hosts events like brewery and restaurant tours, guest speakers, and LEED Green Associates training workshops.

name: SHAC (Student Health Advisory Committee)
description:
Student Health Advisory Committee (SHAC)

 

About

Welcome to the Student Health Advisory Committee or SHAC! The purpose of our group is to promote a healthy campus by serving as liaisons between the University Health Services (UHS) and the general student body. We do this by advising the UHS Executive Director and through our four action groups: Health Equity, Health Navigation, Community Engagement, and Built Environment.

 

SHAC meetings are held weekly each term (Weeks 1-10) and are open to all UO students. Check our Instagram (@UOSHAC) or our weekly emails for updated meeting information. For Spring 2025, we will meet Tuesdays 4-5 PM in the UHS Training Room on the second floor of the UHS building (1590 E. 13th Avenue, across from Oregon Hall).

 

If you are interested in applying to SHAC, applications open at the beginning of each term. You can also attain membership through attending a majority of the meetings in a term. Check out our Instagram or reach out to our email (uhsshac@uoregon.edu) for information about becoming a member. For more information about SHAC and other peer programs at UHS, go to https://health.uoregon.edu/get-involved. In the academic year of 2025-2026, meetings will be on Tuesday, 4-5pm on the second floor of UHS.
summary: The purpose of the Student Health Advisory Committee (SHAC) is to promote health and the University Health Services (UHS) throughout the UO campus community. We do this by advising the UHS director and serving as liaisons between the student body and UHS

name: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority Incorporated
description: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc. was founded January 16, 1920, at Howard University, Washington, D.C. Five coeds envisioned a sorority which would directly affect positive change, chart a course of action for the 1920s and beyond, raise consciousness of their people, encourage the highest standards of scholastic achievement, and foster a greater sense of unity among its members. These women believed that sorority elitism and socializing overshadowed the real mission for progressive organizations and failed to address fully the societal mores, ills, prejudices, and poverty affecting humanity in general and the black community in particular. Since its inception, Zeta has continued its steady climb into the national spotlight with programs designed to demonstrate concern for the human condition both nationally and internationally. The organization has been innovative in that it has chronicled a number of firsts. It was the first National Pan-Hellenic Council organization to centralize its operations in a national headquarters, first to charter a chapter in Africa, first to form auxiliary groups, and first to be constitutionally bound to a fraternity, Phi Beta Sigma Fraternity, Inc. The sorority takes pride in its continued participation in transforming communities through volunteer services from members and its auxiliaries. Zeta Phi Beta has chartered hundreds of chapters worldwide and has a membership of 100,000+. Zeta‘s national and local programs include the endowment of its National Educational Foundation community outreach services and support of multiple affiliate organizations. Zeta chapters and auxiliaries have given untotaled hours of voluntary service to educate the public, assist youth, provide scholarships, support organized charities, and promote legislation for social and civic change. As the sorority moves toward its centennial, it retains its original zest for excellence. It espouses the highest academic ideals and that has resulted in its members serving in groundbreaking roles in all fields of endeavor. Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Inc. is poised for perpetual service to mankind into her second century and beyond.

name: Museum of Natural and Cultural History
description:
The Museum of Natural and Cultural History (MNCH) Student Club, more commonly known as the Sloth Posse, is a hub for students of all majors who are interested in museums, environmental stewardship, science, and fun! New members are always welcomed! Winter Term Meeting held from 6:00-7:00 at the Museum of Natural and Cultural History.

Club members organize campus outreach events, social hours, and more, helping Ducks get inspired about Earth's environments and cultures. As a member, you can:

  • Learn about the museum and all it has to offer 

  • Help increase students’ awareness of the museum and its programs and exhibits

  • Gain valuable experience in museums, community outreach, event planning, and public relations

  • Participate in weekly meetups, special events, and exclusive exhibit openings

  • Make new friends and have fun!

MNCH Student Club formed during fall 2010. Our primary purpose is to provide a direct link between University of Oregon students and MNCH. As such, the MNCH Student Club focus on campus outreach and campus-museum interactions. Operating under established by-laws, the MNCH Student Club meets once per week for one hour to collaborate on projects and provide student input to museum staff.

The MNCH is Oregon's primary repository for anthropological and paleontological collections, housing nearly a million ethnographic and archaeological objects and almost 100,000 fossils and biological specimens from Oregon, the Pacific Northwest, and around the world.

description:
Students for Choice is the pro-choice student-led club at the University of Oregon since the 1970s. The mission of Students for Choice is to create a voice and further the movement for reproductive justice on the University of Oregon campus and in the community at large. We seek to represent a diverse, pro-choice student body as a strong and visible force in our community. In addition, we aim to educate students about current political issues that may threaten their right to make personal reproductive decisions, and to offer tools for countering those forces. It is also a critical goal of Students for Choice to provide a wide range of information to students about gender equitable, locally accessible, and affordable reproductive health care resources. And finally, it is our mission to empower all students to make their own reproductive choices.

 

MEETINGS EVERY WEDNESDAY @6pm in the Women's Center (EMU RM 012)

name: Geology Club UO
description:
We are the University of Oregon Geology Club! We promote a greater understanding of the Geological Sciences through Field Trips, Undergraduate Advising, Poster Exhibits/Information Sessions, Departmental Pancake Breakfasts, and Educational Presentations from career professionals and faculty members.

We focus on raising funds to finance field trips to local or distant geological areas. Every year we plan a field trip during spring break to explore interesting geology and geologic features in surrounding states.

---

In an attempt to enhance an appreciation for the earth and its processes, offer an environment of camaraderie, and provide a source of academic guidance for students interested in the geological sciences, the University of Oregon Geology Club will strive to achieve the following:

Improve the interaction within the Department of Geological Sciences by strengthening the relationship between undergraduates, graduates, and faculty.

Conduct field trips in order to increase knowledge of local and regional geology.

Promote a wider understanding of geology through outreach programs to the University and surrounding community.

Progress toward greater collaboration among the Natural and Physical Sciences.

Introduce undergraduates to graduate and faculty research/field methods.

Share undergraduate research and knowledge.

HAVE FUN while promoting individual growth and group cohesion!

We offer a safe space for all to come learn about our planet!

Sign up for our email list here to be notified about our weekly meetings and upcoming events!
summary:
We're the UO Geology Club! Our weekly meetings involve learning about local and regional geology, undergraduate research/job opportunities, advising, trivia nights (with rock prizes), field trip planning, and free pizza! Hope to see you soon! 
Rock On 🤟

We strive to cultivate a welcoming, inclusive community while building a strong network of students, alumni, faculty, and industry professionals. All majors and identities are welcome to join even if you are not a CS major. 

Our past meetings include technical workshops, study sessions, game nights, industry professionals coming in to guest speak, and more. Our largest annual event is the Tech Together Conference, held each Spring term. This conference brings together industry professionals, department faculty, and recruiters offering members the opportunity to connect with the tech community and explore meaningful conversations around technology. Each year features a new theme; recent themes have included Biases in Big Data and Unconventional Paths in Tech.

We meet every Tuesday from 6-7 PM in Pacific 204. For more information, follow us on Instagram @uo.wics or join our Engage.

description: Delta Sigma Theta Sorority was founded on January 13, 1913 by twenty-two collegiate women at Howard University. These students wanted to use their collective strength to promote academic excellence and to provide assistance to persons in need. The first public act performed by the Delta Founders involved their participation in the Women's Suffrage March in Washington D.C., March 1913. Delta Sigma Theta was incorporated in 1930. The Beta Psi Chapter from the fierce, fabulous, faithful and focused far west region of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated was chartered on March 24, 1945 by 10 women, they are: Joe Anna Young Bradford, Mignon Lilly Cabell, Fannie Stills Chatman, Hattie Fannings Gaskins, Lena Mae Hillsman, Geneva Turner Jordan, June Roe Runnels Key, Lucille Tripplett Morgan, Florence Mills Morton, Ellen Torrence Law. Through the segregation and state laws, these women persevered to introduce Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated to the state of Oregon.

name: ASUO Student Senate
description:
The Student Senate serves as the legislative branch of ASUO, operating a unicameral body responsible for shaping student government policy and making financial decisions. At its core, legislative officers draft, debate, and vote on proposals that influence ASUO’s direction. The Senate is supported by three finance committees, which assist with fund allocation and fiscal decision-making.

The legislative branch consists of 36 ASUO Officers:

  • 23 elected senators: 10 serve on both the Senate and a designated finance committee, while 13 represent specific academic constituencies.
  • 8 elected at-large officers: Assigned to a designated finance committee.
  • 1 at-large officer: Appointed by the Programs Council to serve on the Programs Finance Committee (PFC).
  • 3 officers: Appointed by the ASUO President, confirmed by the Student Senate, and assigned to a finance committee.

Legislative officers serve one- or two-year terms, requiring re-election or re-appointment for continued service. The Senate operates under the ASUO Constitution and its own procedural rules, adhering to university policies, state, and federal laws.

Finance Committees

The three finance committees play a key role in distributing incidental funds during ASUO’s Annual Budget Process. Committee members make informed, viewpoint-neutral decisions and practice responsible financial stewardship. Each committee is led by a chair who oversees meetings and maintains progress. The committees include:

  • Programs Finance Committee (PFC): A 10-member committee recommending the distribution of incidental funds to ASUO’s 200+ Recognized Student Organizations.
  • Departments Finance Committee (DFC): A 6-member committee responsible for recommending funds for university department-based programs.
  • Contracts Finance Committee (CFC): A 6-member committee overseeing fund distribution and crafting legal terms for campus-wide service providers.
summary: The Student Senate is the legislative branch of ASUO, responsible for shaping student government policy and making financial decisions through informed, student-led governance.

name: Craft Center
description:
The UO Craft Center is your on-campus hub for hands-on creativity! Whether you're an experienced artist or just starting out, we provide the space, tools, and guidance to bring your ideas to life. Located in the Erb Memorial Union (EMU), the Craft Center is open to University of Oregon students, faculty, staff, and community members (must be 18 years or older). Our welcoming space encourages learning, exploration, and artistic connection through a variety of programs, workshops, and resources.

What We Offer

Workshops & Studio Orientations
We offer a diverse range of workshops each term, covering ceramics, glasswork, jewelry, woodworking, painting, textiles, photography, and more! Led by experienced instructors and Craft Center student instructors, our workshops are designed to help you learn new skills and create finished projects in just a few sessions.

For those looking to work independently, we provide Studio Orientations, which allow you to gain access to specialized studios after demonstrating basic proficiency in the craft.

Freebie Friday – Drop-In & Get Creative!
Every Friday from 12–2 PM during fall, winter, and spring term, we host Freebie Friday, a completely free drop-in crafting session by the O Desk in the EMU. No registration required—just stop by, grab some supplies, and enjoy a fun, guided craft project. Each week features something different, from rubber stamp carving to embroidery and collage!

The Craft Automat & Craft Kits
Looking for a quick creative fix? The Craft Automat, our gum ball machine filled with mini craft kits and Craft Center swag, is located in the EMU! Turn the crank for $1.00 and get a surprise craft kit to take home.

For more in-depth projects, our Craft Kits provide all the materials and instructions needed to complete a creative project at your own pace. These kits are great for beginners, groups, or anyone looking for an easy way to start crafting. Pricing varies.

Visiting Artist Program
Each term, we bring in a Visiting Artist to share their creative expertise with our community. Through exhibits, artist talks, and hands-on workshops, students get the chance to learn directly from practicing artists. These opportunities are free for UO students.

Gallery Spaces: Craft Center Gallery & Aperture Gallery
The Craft Center is home to two gallery spaces:

The Craft Center Gallery showcases work from our Visiting Artist, students, instructors, and community members throughout the year. The Craft Center Gallery is located on the 2nd floor of the EMU by the Adell McMillan Gallery.

Aperture Gallery focuses on student photography, printmaking, and digital media, giving emerging artists a space to share their work. The Aperture Gallery is located on the 1st floor of the EMU across from US Bank. 

Both galleries are free and open to the public!

DIY Craft Pantry
Need supplies for a small project? Check out the DIY Craft Pantry, our collection of free materials available to UO students. We offer a rotating selection of crafting basics, including crayons, markers, color pencils, letter stamps, acrylic paints, sequins, glue, googly eyes, and more!

Group Activities & Private Events
We offer custom crafting experiences for both student and non-student groups! Whether you're an RA planning a dorm event, a student organization looking for a bonding activity, or a campus department organizing a creative break, we can tailor an event to suit your needs. Non-student groups and off-campus organizations can also schedule workshops with us for team-building or just for fun! For UO students, please fill out this form. For non-UO student groups, please fill out this form.

Materials Requests & Purchasing Supplies
If UO student groups need special materials for a project, you can submit a materials request, and we’ll do our best to provide or source what you need.

In addition to our stocked studio supplies, you can purchase select materials at the Craft Center, including clay, wood, film, photo paper, glass, metal wire and plates, and other assorted tools. Whether you're working on a ceramics piece, a woodworking project, or developing photos, we have essential materials available for purchase to help you bring your ideas to life!

Get Involved!
With so many ways to engage—whether through workshops, drop-in events, galleries, or independent studio work—the Craft Center is here to support your creativity. Follow us on social media or check our website for the latest updates on events, registration dates, and more!

Come visit, create something amazing, and be part of our vibrant artistic community! ✨

To see all our current offerings, please go to craftcenter.uoregon.edu.
summary: The Craft Center offers a comprehensive arts program open to UO students, faculty and staff, as well as Eugene community members. The Center is both educational and recreational, and encourages all levels of interest, from beginning hobbyist to serious...

name: Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Inc
description: Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority was founded on January 15, 1908 on the campus of Howard University in Washington, DC. Its founders were among the fewer than 1,000 African Americans enrolled in higher education institutions in 1908 and the 25 women who received Bachelor of Arts degrees from Howard University between 1908 and 1911. Nine juniors and seniors who constituted the initial core group of founding members and seven sophomores who were extended an invitation for membership without initiation comprised what are acknowledged as Alpha Kappa Alpha’s original 16 founders. Led by Ethel Hedgeman (Lyle), the nine Howard University students who came together to form Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority were the scholastic leaders of their classes. Each also had a special talent or gift that further enhanced the potential of this dynamic group. The Sigma Delta Chapter of Alpha Kappa Alpha, Sorority Incorporated was chartered at the University of Oregon on April 30, 2000. Prior to that date members were part of general membership.

Ducks and Dragons aims to be an outlet for those that lack the means to participate in Dungeons and Dragons in a university setting and find people that they can truly connect to.

For the Spring 2025 term, we are meeting every Tuesday from 6pm to 9pm in the Knight Library's DREAM Lab. Please join our Discord, where we announce any new events. You can join by clicking on the little world icon below this text.
summary: Ducks and Dragons is a collective of students that aims to grow a devout community through a shared love of Tabletop Games.We strive to increase accessibility and community participation by the medium of tabletop games.

description: Pre-SOMA aims to promote Osteopathic medicine, increase the number of applicants to Osteopathic medical schools, support aspiring Osteopathic physicians at all levels of education, and continue the proud legacy of Osteopathic Medicine. Our goal is to mentor pre-medical students by providing resources and connections to help them succeed in their journey to medical school. We provide students monthly opportunities to attend Osteopathic Physicians and Surgeons of Oregon(OPSO) events at the medical school in Lebanon where students interact with physicians and medical students.
summary: Pre-SOMA aims to promote Osteopathic medicine, increase the number of applicants to Osteopathic medical schools, support aspiring Osteopathic physicians at all levels of education, and continue the proud legacy of Osteopathic Medicine. Our goal is to m...

name: ASUO Executive
description:
The ASUO Executive Branch serves as the central hub of student governance, managing daily operations, driving student initiatives, and overseeing more than 200 recognized student organizations. It advocates for student interests with decision-makers on and off campus, including at the state and federal levels. Executive Branch Officers receive guidance from the Director of Student Government Engagement and Success.

At its core, the Executive Branch consists of a 15- to 25-person cabinet, including key roles such as the president, vice president, chief of staff, directors, secretaries, and election commissioners. While the president (and often the vice president) is elected, the remaining cabinet members are appointed by the president. Executive officers serve under the ASUO Constitution, adhere to university policies, and comply with state and federal laws. Their terms are limited to one year, requiring re-election or re-appointment for continued service.

The Elections Board, a semi-independent component of the Executive Branch, oversees ASUO’s Spring Election. It consists of four Commissioners and a Coordinator, all appointed annually, confirmed by the Student Senate, and serving limited-duration terms. To maintain impartiality, the Elections Board operates separately from the Executive Cabinet.
summary: The ASUO Executive Branch serves as the central hub of student governance, managing operations, advocating for student interests, and overseeing more than 200 recognized student organizations.

name: Slam Poetry UO
description: The UO Poetry Slam fosters a community that appreciates and participates in poetic expression of all kinds and at all levels. We host open mic nights, workshops, slams, and featured poets. The UO Poetry slam is open to all UO students, and every fall, hosts a Slam to select a team to send to the College Unions Poetry Slam Invitational.
summary: The UO Poetry Slam fosters a community that appreciates and participates in poetic expression of all kinds and at all levels. We host open mic nights, workshops, slams, and featured poets. The UO Poetry slam is open to all UO students, and every fall, ...

name: Oregon Mindfulness
description: UO Mindfulness is a student organization that explores various meditative practices and areas of the human experience. In our weekly meetings, we typically have a topic of discussion related to life (such as love, self-talk, or happiness) and follow it up with a group meditation. Whether you've come for stress reduction, increased self-awareness, improvements in mental health, a community of like-minded peers, or just a space to sit and be, feel free to stop by. It's a wonderful place to find authentic human connection. You are welcome here!
summary: UO Mindfulness is a student organization that explores various meditative practices and areas of the human experience. It's a great place to heal and form meaningful and authentic relationships.

name: LiveMove - University of Oregon Transportation and Livability Student Group
description: LiveMove, the University of Oregon Transportation and Livability Student Group, brings together undergraduate and graduate students from varied backgrounds and disciplines to focus on the planning and designing of transportation systems as they relate to community quality of life and livability. LiveMove’s mission statement is: “To promote healthy, sustainable communities by integrating transportation and livability through collaboration, education, research, and outreach.” Participating in the group allows students to collaborate on planning and design projects, gain leadership skills, bring in high-profile guest speakers, and network. In addition, generous funding is available from local community organizations and agencies to support UO students working on projects relating to active transportation and liveability. This group is open to all majors, undergraduates, and graduates!

description:
Chinese Student Association (CSA) is a student-led organization that serves, supports, and educates students at the University of Oregon. CSA provides inclusive spaces that encourage meaningful cultural exploration and connection within the Chinese community. Through networking and social events, CSA promotes and showcases Chinese identity and diasporic cultural awareness. The organization engages students through educational and culturally focused activities that introduce traditions and history rooted deeply in Chinese heritage.

description: UO SACNAS exists to create a supportive community of STEM students at the University of Oregon. SACNAS is the largest multicultural and multidisciplinary National Organization and stand for the Society for Advancement of Chicanos/Hispanics and Native Americans in Science. Our chapter organizes and participates in a variety of activities related to STEM research, professional development, outreach, and community-building. Our mission is to advance the success of Chicano, Hispanic, and Native American students in obtaining advanced degrees, careers, leadership positions, and equality in the STEM field. Students of all backgrounds are welcome to join our community in support of this mission.
